description: Disaster recovery navigation: FEMA Individual Assistance application and appeals, SBA disaster loans, D-SNAP activation, insurance claim disputes, and contractor fraud red flags.
sources: 44 CFR Parts 206 and 212 (FEMA Individual Assistance regulations)
SBA Disaster Loan Program 13 CFR Part 123
State Department of Insurance complaint procedures
National Insurance Crime Bureau (NICB) contractor fraud alerts

triples.subject: FEMA Individual Assistance (IA)
triples.object: Housing Assistance (HA) for temporary housing or home repair, and Other Needs Assistance (ONA) for personal property, medical, dental, childcare, and moving costs

triples.subject: FEMA IA application
triples.object: DisasterAssistance.gov, FEMA app, or 1-800-621-3362; apply as soon as disaster declaration is made — do not wait for damage assessment

triples.subject: FEMA IA application deadline
triples.object: 60 days from the date of major disaster declaration; check disaster-specific registration period — some are extended; missing deadline is one of the most common errors

triples.subject: FEMA IA application
triples.object: SSN (or SSN of minor child), address of damaged property, insurance information (even if denied or insufficient), contact phone, direct deposit info for faster payment

triples.subject: FEMA IA denial
triples.object: (1) insufficient damage documented, (2) insurance coverage available, (3) unable to verify occupancy or ownership, (4) address not in disaster area, (5) duplicate application

triples.subject: FEMA IA appeal
triples.object: 60 days from date on denial letter to submit written appeal; do not miss this window — reinstatement after 60 days requires proof of good cause

triples.subject: FEMA IA appeal
triples.object: write appeal letter explaining why decision is wrong; attach supporting documents (deed, lease, photos, contractor estimates, insurance denial letter); mail or upload to DisasterAssistance.gov

triples.subject: FEMA IA appeal
triples.object: appeals are frequently successful — inspectors may have been unable to access property, documentation was missing, or damage was underestimated; always appeal a denial

triples.subject: FEMA insurance requirement
triples.object: FEMA requires use of insurance proceeds first; if insured, you must file an insurance claim and provide documentation of outcome before FEMA will cover unmet needs

triples.subject: FEMA IA housing assistance
triples.object: maximum Housing Assistance award adjusted annually; as of 2024 approximately $43,900 for home repair/replacement; check FEMA.gov for current fiscal year cap

triples.subject: FEMA Transitional Sheltering Assistance (TSA)
triples.object: hotel/motel housing directly paid by FEMA for displaced disaster survivors who cannot return home; requires IA eligibility determination first

triples.subject: SBA disaster loan
triples.object: homeowners (up to $500,000 for real property), renters and homeowners (up to $100,000 for personal property), and businesses (up to $2M for economic injury or physical damage)

triples.subject: SBA disaster loan
triples.object: homeowners: approximately 2.75–4% depending on income and credit; businesses: approximately 4%; rates vary by disaster — check SBA disaster declaration announcement

triples.subject: SBA disaster loan
triples.object: SBA.gov/disaster or 1-800-659-2955; apply even if you don't want a loan — SBA denial may be required to unlock FEMA Other Needs Assistance (ONA) grants

triples.subject: SBA disaster loan denial
triples.object: if denied SBA loan for creditworthiness reasons, FEMA may provide additional ONA grant assistance; this is a known pathway — apply to SBA even with poor credit

triples.subject: D-SNAP (Disaster SNAP)
triples.object: emergency food benefits for households in presidentially declared disaster areas who do not normally receive SNAP; one-time or short-term benefit

triples.subject: D-SNAP eligibility
triples.object: disaster-net-income limit is higher than regular SNAP; includes households up to the gross income limit of the SNAP program plus disaster-related expenses (food loss, home repair costs)

triples.subject: D-SNAP
triples.object: state must request D-SNAP from USDA FNS after major disaster declaration; not available in all disasters — check USDA FNS disaster assistance page for active activations

triples.subject: homeowners insurance claim
triples.object: file promptly (within days not weeks — policies have prompt notice requirements); photograph all damage before cleanup; get claim number and adjuster name in writing

triples.subject: insurance claim dispute
triples.object: request itemized denial letter explaining basis; ask for supervisor review; request complete claim file under your state's right-to-examine laws

triples.subject: insurance claim dispute
triples.object: public adjusters represent policyholders (not insurers) on a contingency fee (10–15% of settlement); useful for complex or large claims; find through National Association of Public Insurance Adjusters (NAPIA)

triples.subject: insurance claim dispute
triples.object: file complaint with state Department of Insurance if insurer unreasonably delays or denies; most states require insurers to acknowledge claim within 10 days and decide within 30–45 days

triples.subject: insurance bad faith
triples.object: insurer acts in bad faith by unreasonably denying valid claim, delaying payment without cause, or failing to investigate; gives rise to bad faith lawsuit for damages beyond policy limits in most states

triples.subject: contractor fraud red flags
triples.object: (1) door-to-door solicitation immediately after disaster, (2) demands large upfront cash payment, (3) no local address or license number, (4) pressures you to sign immediately, (5) offers to waive your insurance deductible

triples.subject: contractor fraud protection
triples.object: verify license with state contractor licensing board; require written contract with scope, price, and timeline; never pay more than 10–30% upfront; file complaint with NICB at 800-TEL-NICB if fraud suspected

triples.subject: disaster legal aid
triples.object: many state bar associations activate disaster legal aid hotlines after major disasters; American Bar Association provides free legal help at disasterlegalhelp.org; FEMA may fund legal aid through LSC

triples.subject: FEMA hazard mitigation grant
triples.object: after disaster, FEMA Hazard Mitigation Grant Program (HMGP) may fund elevation, retrofitting, or buyout of flood-prone homes; contact state emergency management agency — separate from IA grants
